tests.title: Curiosity in Attribute Pipeline — Response Quality
tests.implemented: Attribute pipeline now uses unified CURIOSITY_INTENT_PROMPT (3 beats with closing question) instead of removed CURIOSITY_ATTRIBUTE_RESPONSE_PROMPT (2 beats, no question).
tests.scenario: Object: tiger | Attribute: body color | Child: 'Why do tigers have stripes?' | Age: 6

params.activity_target: noticing and describing what a tiger looks like
params.last_model_response: What do you notice about this tiger's fur?

checks.criterion: Contains a question mark (BEAT 3 closing question)
Does NOT say 'That's a great question'
Mentions stripes or camouflage (answers the question)

tests.title: Curiosity in Attribute Pipeline — Safety
tests.implemented: Unified curiosity prompt in attribute pipeline must still respect SENSORY_SAFETY_RULES (no touch/smell/taste invitations).
tests.scenario: Object: cactus | Attribute: texture | Child: 'Can I touch the cactus?' | Age: 5

params.last_model_response: What do you notice about the cactus?

checks.criterion: Suggests a safe visual or imaginative alternative

tests.title: Correct Answer in Attribute Pipeline — Follow-up Regression
tests.implemented: Verify that follow-up question generation still works for intents that receive follow-ups (e.g., CORRECT_ANSWER) after the curiosity unification.
tests.scenario: Object: apple | Attribute: body color | Child correctly answered about apple color

params.response_text: Yes! Apples can be red, green, or yellow. Some even have stripes!

messages.content: What color do you think this apple is?
Yes! Apples can be red, green, or yellow. Some even have stripes!

params.attribute_soft_guide:
SENSORY SAFETY (applies to all sensory invitations):
- Default to sight: 'What do you notice?', 'Which part looks the biggest?', 'Is it shiny or dull?'
- Allow imagination/guessing: 'Do you think it would feel rough?', 'If it rolled, would it go fast or slow?'
- Do NOT invite the child to TOUCH, SMELL, TASTE, LICK, or PHYSICALLY INTERACT with the object —
  the environment may be unsafe (parks, public spaces, unknown items).
- Do NOT use 'Do you know…' framing — it creates testing pressure.
- For imitation: only voices and stretches/movements are OK ('Let's bark like a puppy!').
  NEVER suggest petting an animal, touching/smelling a plant, or any physical contact.

SUGGESTED EXPLORATION DIRECTION: body color
ACTIVITY GOAL: noticing colors

When choosing your follow-up question, you can gently lean toward
body color when it fits naturally. You do NOT need to force it.

THREE TECHNIQUES (use ONE per turn, when it fits):

A) SALIENCE — include a body color-related sensory word in the
   question itself, so the attribute feels naturally present without
   adding any lead-in before the question.

B) FRAME WEAVING — when the child noticed something OTHER than
   body color, offer a choice or comparison that includes
   body color as one option.

C) NATURAL BRIDGE — when YOU feel the child has explored body color
   with enough depth for a natural activity invitation, extend toward
   the activity goal.

EVIDENCE REQUIREMENT: Your REASON: line MUST include at least one direct
quote from the child's actual messages in this conversation, enclosed in
double quotes (").

TRANSITION SIGNAL: When you choose technique C and include an
activity-preview question, your output should be exactly:
1. one child-facing question
2. then on a new line: [ACTIVITY_READY]
3. then on a new line: REASON: <1-sentence explanation with a direct child quote>

ANTI-PATTERNS — NEVER produce these:
✗ 'What body color is it?' — that's a quiz
✗ 'Do you know what body color it has?' — quiz with wrapper
✗ 'What else can you tell me about it?' — too vague, no direction
✗ 'Let's look at its body color!' — forced redirect

checks.criterion: Contains a question mark (single follow-up question)
Does NOT invite touch, smell, or taste

tests.title: Curiosity in Attribute Pipeline — Off-Topic Question
tests.implemented: When child asks an unrelated curiosity question in the attribute pipeline, the unified prompt should answer it without forcing an artificial attribute connection.
tests.scenario: Object: tiger | Attribute: body color | Child: 'How do planes fly?' | Age: 6

params.activity_target: noticing and describing what a tiger looks like
params.last_model_response: What do you notice about this tiger's fur?

checks.criterion: Answers the child's question about planes
Does NOT force an artificial connection to body color
Does NOT say 'That's a great question'
Contains a question mark (BEAT 3 closing question)
